front: Figure: fortieth president of the United States, in office 1981-1989
Figure: nicknamed "the Great Communicator" for his speaking prowess, and "the Gipper" from an earlier acting role
Figure: a Republican president whose second term is frequently described as "tarnished" by a major arms-and-hostages scandal
Figure: served as president of the Screen Actors Guild

Event: in 1947, this man testified before HUAC about Screen Actors Guild literature tied to a "Committee for a Far-Eastern Democratic Policy," and under the FBI codename "T-10" secretly informed on suspected communist sympathizers
Event: as a celebrity spokesman, recorded an LP for the American Medical Association's anti-Medicare campaign, titled "Speaks Out Against Socialized Medicine"
Event: this man's political debut came with the "A Time for Choosing" speech in support of Barry Goldwater's 1964 campaign
Figure: won the California governorship in 1966 after incumbent Pat Brown's response to the Watts riots was perceived as weak, and won a second term by defeating "Big Daddy" Unruh
Event: as California governor, this man put down the People's Park protest on "Bloody Thursday" and largely wiped out predecessor Pat Brown's budget deficit
Law: this 1967 gun-control law, signed by Reagan as governor, became national news after armed Black Panthers occupied the California State Capitol to protest it
Law: this pre-Roe law, signed by Reagan in his first year as California governor, liberalized the state's abortion statute
Law: Governor Reagan signed this act, making it much harder to involuntarily commit the mentally ill

Event: at the Neshoba County Fair, this man declared his belief in "states' rights"; Paul Krugman later attributed the phrase's use to "tacit race-baiting," and this man repeatedly invoked welfare-fraud figure Linda Taylor during the same campaign

Event: aided by strategist Kevin Phillips's "Southern strategy" and foreign-policy adviser Jeane Kirkpatrick, this man defeated Jimmy Carter in the 1980 election, retorting "There you go again" during a debate
Figure: this man's 1980 campaign was the first presidential campaign to earn an NRA endorsement
Event: this man defeated Walter Mondale and running mate Geraldine Ferraro in a 1984 landslide (49 states), running ads about a "bear in the woods" and declaring it "morning again in America"

Event: this man's first speech as president concluded with a story about Martin Treptow, a soldier who died fighting in World War I; the address (written by Ken Khachigian) declared "government is not the solution to our problems; government is the problem"

Event: this man was shot by John Hinckley Jr. outside the Washington Hilton Hotel, an attack Hinckley carried out to impress actress Jodie Foster, whom he had become obsessed with after watching Taxi Driver
Figure: this press secretary was shot and left permanently paralyzed during the 1981 assassination attempt on Reagan, prompting Secretary of State Alexander Haig to declare "I am in control here"
Event: this man reportedly joked "Honey, I forgot to duck" to wife Nancy after being shot in the 1981 assassination attempt
Law: the shooting of press secretary James Brady eventually led to this 1993 act, which mandated federal background checks on firearm purchases
Event: this man signed the Kemp-Roth tax cut into law and enacted his deregulatory economic policy with the help of OMB Director David Stockman
Event: this president's administration saw the first deployment of Peacekeeper missiles, the Gulf of Sidra incident, and the beginning of the Savings and Loan crisis
Figure: this Interior Secretary under Reagan controversially banned a Grass Roots and Beach Boys concert on the National Mall

Event: Reagan defused this movement to free up federally-held Western land by appointing James Watt as Interior Secretary, having declared himself a "sagebrush rebel" while campaigning in Salt Lake City

Event: despite having received the union's endorsement in 1980, this man broke this union's strike in 1981 by firing most of the nation's air traffic controllers after a 48-hour warning, imposing a lifetime civil-service ban later lifted by Bill Clinton
Figure: this president escalated the War on Drugs against Black communities during his two terms
Figure: chaired Reagan's Commission on Civil Rights and claimed comparable worth was "the looniest idea since Looney Tunes"
Figure: Reagan's Supreme Court nomination of this judge was attacked by Ted Kennedy, who claimed "blacks would sit at segregated lunch counters" if he were confirmed

Event: Reagan pursued this policy of limited engagement with apartheid South Africa (formulated by adviser Chester Crocker), controversially meeting with UNITA leader Jonas Savimbi and vetoing the more punitive Dellums Amendment
Event: this president controversially placed a wreath at this German military cemetery, which included graves of SS members, and claimed the SS were "also victims" of the Nazis
Event: this man joked about food aid to the poor being "botulism ridden," mocking the Symbionese Liberation Army's demand for food distribution after the kidnapping of Patty Hearst
Event: at the Brandenburg Gate in West Berlin, this man told Mikhail Gorbachev, "If you seek liberalization... tear down this wall!"
work: Cold War Diplomacy with the Soviet Union

front: Event: this man called the Soviet Union an "evil empire" and predicted it would be relegated to the "ash heap of history"
work: Cold War Diplomacy with the Soviet Union

front: Event: this man proposed a controversial missile-defense program using lasers and particle beams, which he clashed over with Gorbachev at their summit negotiations
work: Cold War Diplomacy with the Soviet Union

front: Event: this president blasted the Soviet Union after it shot down this passenger airliner, Korean Air Lines Flight 007, in 1983
work: Cold War Diplomacy with the Soviet Union

front: Event: negotiations between Reagan and Gorbachev stalled at this 1986 summit over the Brilliant Pebbles component of SDI; Suzanne Massie had suggested Reagan use the phrase "trust, but verify" with Gorbachev
work: Cold War Diplomacy with the Soviet Union

front: Event: Reagan's hawkish rhetoric and SDI program contributed to Soviet paranoia during this 1983 NATO exercise, which came close to being mistaken for a real first-strike preparation
work: Cold War Diplomacy with the Soviet Union

front: Event: this president ordered the 1983 invasion of this island nation (Operation Urgent Fury), overthrowing coup leader Hudson Austin after the execution of Prime Minister Maurice Bishop
Event: this president ordered 1986 air strikes on Libya (Operation El Dorado Canyon) in retaliation for the bombing of a Berlin discotheque

Figure: this Reagan staffer, along with Robert McFarlane and witness Fawn Hall, helped run the scheme selling arms to Iran and diverting proceeds to fund the Contras, circumventing the Boland Amendment
Event: this president's Iran-Contra scheme diverted arms-sale proceeds to fund rebels fighting Daniel Ortega's Sandinista government in this country

Event: Iranian intermediaries Manouchehr Ghorbanifar and Mehdi Hashemi were involved in this scandal; Hashemi leaked news of the Boland Amendment breach, and Reagan later admitted "mistakes were made"
Event: a supply plane flown by this man, ferrying aid to the Contras, was shot down, dealing a major diplomatic blow to the Reagan administration
Figure: this Reagan Defense Secretary was later indicted (and resigned) over the Iran-Contra scandal, which broke during Reagan's second term
Event: in Guatemala, this president endorsed dictator Efrain Rios Montt as a "man of great integrity" with a "bum rap" a day before the Dos Erres massacre
Event: this president's administration gave military support to a regime in El Salvador responsible for this massacre
Event: this incoming president's reputation was boosted when the Iran Hostage Crisis ended on his Inauguration Day

Law: this 1986 federal law, signed by Reagan, paradoxically included Representative William Hughes's amendment banning civilian sales of newly manufactured machine guns; a handwritten Reagan note flipped Representative Scott Klug's vote to narrowly pass it
Event: Washington National Airport was renamed for this president
Work: this Tony Kushner play is set during Reagan's presidential administration, portrayed there as having restored the country's "sacred position among nations"
Work: this J. G. Ballard short story from The Atrocity Exhibition is a mock scientific paper about Reagan's sexual appeal
back: "Why I Want to Fuck Ronald Reagan" (J. G. Ballard)

front: Figure: this First Lady infamously consulted a personal astrologer named Joan Quigley and coined the anti-drug slogan "Just Say No"
